author: @jamesdaley3223
text:
I had a thought and couldn’t figure out how to post a general message so,  I figured this is a video close to the same topic   
What if, instead of using a mechanical system to modulate your turbine blades to sort of “load shed” to deal with high winds, you could integrate a sort of braking coil that could handle the higher output and charge a separate capacitor bank or something for supplying a high demand load later. It would keep your turbine from over rotating and make use of the force to do so. Not to mention allowing a more stable output overall. 
Although, I suppose it wouldn’t help the lateral stress to your shaft and keep it from wanting to fly away.
I look forward to experiencing your take on the idea if you happen to decide it’s worth thinking about.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Luke has won.
It's an awesome video but I would say you have wind turbines pretty much covered now. I wonder, with an arduino, could you spin a magnetic marble 10000 rpm ? I always wanted to try that but lack time. Bad excuse ... I know .. Would gain a lot of views I guess ...Just an idea ... In my experience I think it's possibe. Maybe more or far more. Maybe destroy the marble ...

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: with rotating fields you can do this with a magnetised steel ball-bearing (even an un-magnetised one will work). ramp up the rotation/frequency of the fields and you can get incredibly high rpm. 10,000 rpm is child's play. but be careful, very high rpm stores a lot of energy. If your 'marble' fails at high rpm make sure you have very strong catchment. also try it with helium shroud and/or vacuum. note; with rotating fields you can also levitate the ball. I don't know what the limit rpm is but my college technicians did this in 1990 with various tiny metal discs and rings up to the burst limits of the materials. i don't have the notes anymore but i remember the rpms were into very silly numbers.

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 Thanks. Levitating in a vacuum would get rid of all losses i think. I might try it someday. Fun ....

author: @401ksolar
text: The one Luke built is similar to the one I've had for years, however I have a 6-in 3/8 thick pipe in the middle as a flywheel with heavy bearings top and bottom it will create 100 amps at 12 volts with a 20 to 30 mph wind it seems to top out at 30 mph there's no need for brakes this thing will not spin up too fast, however for safety sake it should be beefed up quite a bit to stay operating as long as mine.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
1200 watts! what!? is that correct? then again, 20-30mph wind. hmm?
what are the details of generator/gearing etc? thanks.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 it is geared up to about 7:00 to 1:00, using a chain, the generator is a permanent magnet 36 volt DC motor with brushes, it is a drive motor recycled from a Clark floor cleaning machine, yes it will do 100 amps to the charge controller in good wind conditions, it doesn't seem to exceed 12 volts below 20 mph winds, so this thing shines in big wind storms, that's why this design was used by Arctic weather watchers

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: Do you have any videos or other documentation on your turbine anywhere? Highly interesting project!

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @Teknopottu well I have no videos of it, however I do have stills of it during the building process, this year it will be getting new bearings in an updated rotor design, they do have all the information, anything you want to know about it just ask.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text:
@401ksolar Great! Sorry for english not being my primary language (I'm from Finland) so I'll try to make sense.

 What kind of ratio did you use to weld barrels together? Luke welded the halves about 50/50 of each other.

Do you use one or several barrels? I've now got two matching barrels and been thinking about joining an oil drum savonius on top of another at 90° rotational angle.

What kind of bearings would you recommend? The force is diagonal on the bearings so not every type works for too long.

What kind of electronics do you use to level the voltage? Charge controller? Do you charge battery packs and of what kind?

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, the vertical turbine shaft is too small in diameter. You can see how it deflects as it turns. You should have used a stiff iron pipe for the shaft and had the allthread rods welded at the ends of the pipe. The pipe wouldn't deflect, nor the short stub in each bearing. It would make it more efficient. The other drum looks wild!

replies.author: @iliascoandas2296
replies.text: - An answer to Pirate Labs : If a finger can press the generator (the lighter), then the same can be done by an elastic metal sheet (plate). You also have to stick a cable as the second pole to the lower metallic end , but very carefully because there is plastic part behind the metal , so it is very sensitive.

author: @montynorth3009
text:
I like the Savonius design because I expect it will deliver more power.
I wonder if just butting up the 2 halves on their cut edges rather than overlapping would be better because the scoop area would be increased. 
A bar or plate across top and bottom could provide the rigidity.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: Monty. I made up a test jig many years ago which would allow me to adjust the distance of overlap of 2  of these 1/2 drums and test for energy efficiency. I found that butt joining was not the best . It turns out that a slight overlap around 1/3 was the best actually and was where the peak efficiency of conversion of wind to electrical energy was found. I guess that this was due to the back of the drum slowing down the movement as it goes into the wind. With a slight overlap the wind that goes into the one side gets pushed into the second half through the overlap in the middle and this forces the curved side forwards against the wind. Not sure if I am explaining it correctly??

replies.author: @dogdooish
replies.text: Butt join leaves nowhere for the air to go  :)

replies.author: @montynorth3009
replies.text:
@bartronicsecurity Good information.
Thanks.
